revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niiname-sai]][[de:Niiname-sai]][[en:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[fr:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[id:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[ja:新嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (11월)]][[simple:Niiname-no-Matsuri]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niiname-sai]][[de:Niiname-sai]][[en:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[fr:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[id:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[ja:新嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (11월)]][[simple:Niiname-no-Matsuri]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiname-sai]][[de:Niiname-sai]][[en:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[fr:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[id:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[ja:新嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (11월)]][[simple:Niiname-no-Matsuri]] {{Short description|Japanese harvest ritual}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Infobox holiday | holiday_name = Niiname-no-Matsuri | type = | image = Niiname-sai.png | imagesize = | caption = | official_name = | nickname = | observedby = Japan | litcolor = | longtype = Religious | significance = {{ill|Harvest festival|en|Harvest festival|WD=Q2304773|lt=harvest ritual|1=Harvest festival}} | date = November 23 | scheduling = same day each year | celebrations = | observances = | relatedto = {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}}, [[Daijosai]] }} The '''''Niiname-sai''''' (新嘗祭, also read '''Shinjō-sai''' and '''Niiname-no-Matsuri''') is a Japanese {{ill|Harvest festival|en|Harvest festival|WD=Q2304773|lt=harvest ritual|1=Harvest festival}}.{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}The ritual is celebrated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, who thanks the [[Shinto]] deities for a prosperous year and prays for a fruitful new year. It takes place near the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries]] in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]] and at several large {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s. The first Niiname-sai for a new emperor is known as the [[Daijosai|Daijō-sai]] (大嘗祭), and is part of his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]].
In pre-modern Japan, the date of the Niiname-sai was moveable, taking place on the last {{ill|Rabbit (zodiac)|en|Rabbit (zodiac)|WD=Q844723|lt=Day of the Rabbit|1=Rabbit (zodiac)}} of the eleventh month of the old Japanese lunar calendar, but in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date was fixed at {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}}, and this date became a national holiday, {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}}, in the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}} after World War II.
The Engishiki specified imperial involvement with four festivals, the [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]], the two [[Ōharae-shiki]]s and Niiname-no-Matsuri for tribute.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016-11-11 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |edition=Illustrated |location=New York |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|36}}
In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and Niiname-sai in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Ceremony == During the Niiname-sai, an ancient Shinto ritual that says thanks for the crops of the previous year{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and prays for fruitfulness in the following year,{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} says thanks to his [[kami|gods]] for the fall harvest. It is held in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]],{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} as well as other shrines including [[Ise Grand Shrine]]{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}
== Date == Traditionally, it was held on the last {{ill|Rabbit (zodiac)|en|Rabbit (zodiac)|WD=Q844723|lt=Day of the Rabbit|1=Rabbit (zodiac)}} in the eleventh month of the [[Japanese calendar|old lunar calendar]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}
Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date has been fixed on {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which corresponds to the modern public holiday {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which was introduced in 1948.
As a ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|WD=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}'', the name of the ritual is associated with {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter|1=Winter}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998a}}
== Name == ''Niiname-sai'' is the common name of the festival, but the same kanji can also be read ''Jinshō-sai''{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} or ''Niiname-no-Matsuri''.{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} ''Niiname'' can also be read ''Niinae'', ''Niinai'', ''Niwanai'', ''Niwanami'' or ''Nyūnami''.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典">{{Cite web|url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68770|title = 國學院大學 デジタルミュージアム}}</ref>
The first Niiname-sai following the accession of a new emperor is called the ''[[Enthronement of the Japanese Emperor#The Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]]'' (大嘗祭, also read ''Ōname-Matsuri'' and ''Ōnie-no-Matsuri'').{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998}}<!-- Britannica also verifies this, but not the readings, and Britannica's separate article 大嘗祭 actually doesn't give the reading おおなめまつり. -->
== In literature == Book 19 of the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' includes six poems (numbered 4273 to 4278) composed on the 25th day of the eleventh month of 752, the "''Niiname-kai'' poems".<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/> The "''nyūnami''" is alluded to in one of the ''[[azuma-uta]]'' (songs of eastern Japan) included in Book 14.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/>
== See also == * [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Japanese Imperial Rituals]] * [[:en:Saiten|Saiten]]
==Notes== {{notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten |title = Niiname-sai |language = japanese |year = 2014 |publisher = [[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/新嘗祭-109054#E3.83.96.E3.83.AA.E3.82.BF.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.E5.9B.BD.E9.9A.9B.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8.20.E5.B0.8F.E9.A0.85.E7.9B.AE.E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|2014}} }} * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Daijisen]] |title = Shinjō-sai |language = japanese |year = 1998<!-- Couldn't find a date. The edition consulted was the one on Kotobank. Gave it the same date as is cited in the "Ariwara no Narihira" article. --> |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/新嘗祭-109054#E3.83.87.E3.82.B8.E3.82.BF.E3.83.AB.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E6.B3.89 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Digital Daijisen|1998a}} }} * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Daijisen]] |title = Daijō-sai |language = japanese |year = 1998<!-- Couldn't find a date. The edition consulted was the one on Kotobank. Gave it the same date as is cited in the "Ariwara no Narihira" article. --> |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/大嘗祭-39450#E3.83.87.E3.82.B8.E3.82.BF.E3.83.AB.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E6.B3.89 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Digital Daijisen|1998b}} }}

The Engishiki specified imperial involvement with four festivals, the [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]], the two [[Ōharae-shiki]]s and Niiname-no-Matsuri for tribute.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016-11-11 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |edition=Illustrated |location=New York |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|36}}
In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and Niiname-sai in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>

It is held in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]],{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} as well as other shrines including [[Ise Grand Shrine]]{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}

Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date has been fixed on {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which corresponds to the modern public holiday {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which was introduced in 1948.
As a ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|WD=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}'', the name of the ritual is associated with {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter|1=Winter}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998a}}
== Name == ''Niiname-sai'' is the common name of the festival, but the same kanji can also be read ''Jinshō-sai''{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} or ''Niiname-no-Matsuri''.{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} ''Niiname'' can also be read ''Niinae'', ''Niinai'', ''Niwanai'', ''Niwanami'' or ''Nyūnami''.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典">{{Cite web|url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68770|title = 國學院大學 デジタルミュージアム}}</ref>

Gave it the same date as is cited in the "Ariwara no Narihira" article. --> |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/大嘗祭-39450#E3.83.87.E3.82.B8.E3.82.BF.E3.83.AB.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E6.B3.89 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Digital Daijisen|1998b}} }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niiname-sai]][[de:Niiname-sai]][[en:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[fr:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[id:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[ja:新嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (11월)]][[simple:Niiname-no-Matsuri]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niiname-sai]][[de:Niiname-sai]][[en:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[fr:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[id:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[ja:新嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (11월)]][[simple:Niiname-no-Matsuri]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Niiname-sai]][[de:Niiname-sai]][[en:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[fr:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[id:Niiname-no-Matsuri]][[ja:新嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (11월)]][[simple:Niiname-no-Matsuri]] {{Short description|Japanese harvest ritual}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Infobox holiday | holiday_name = Niiname-no-Matsuri | type = | image = Niiname-sai.png | imagesize = | caption = | official_name = | nickname = | observedby = Japan | litcolor = | longtype = Religious | significance = {{ill|Harvest festival|en|Harvest festival|WD=Q2304773|lt=harvest ritual|1=Harvest festival}} | date = November 23 | scheduling = same day each year | celebrations = | observances = | relatedto = {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}}, [[Daijosai]] }} The '''''Niiname-sai''''' (新嘗祭, also read '''Shinjō-sai''' and '''Niiname-no-Matsuri''') is a Japanese {{ill|Harvest festival|en|Harvest festival|WD=Q2304773|lt=harvest ritual|1=Harvest festival}}.{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}The ritual is celebrated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, who thanks the [[Shinto]] deities for a prosperous year and prays for a fruitful new year. It takes place near the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries]] in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]] and at several large {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s. The first Niiname-sai for a new emperor is known as the [[Daijosai|Daijō-sai]] (大嘗祭), and is part of his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremonies]].
In pre-modern Japan, the date of the Niiname-sai was moveable, taking place on the last {{ill|Rabbit (zodiac)|en|Rabbit (zodiac)|WD=Q844723|lt=Day of the Rabbit|1=Rabbit (zodiac)}} of the eleventh month of the old Japanese lunar calendar, but in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date was fixed at {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}}, and this date became a national holiday, {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}}, in the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}} after World War II.
The Engishiki specified imperial involvement with four festivals, the [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]], the two [[Ōharae-shiki]]s and Niiname-no-Matsuri for tribute.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016-11-11 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |edition=Illustrated |location=New York |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|36}}
In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and Niiname-sai in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Ceremony == During the Niiname-sai, an ancient Shinto ritual that says thanks for the crops of the previous year{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and prays for fruitfulness in the following year,{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} says thanks to his [[kami|gods]] for the fall harvest. It is held in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]],{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} as well as other shrines including [[Ise Grand Shrine]]{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}
== Date == Traditionally, it was held on the last {{ill|Rabbit (zodiac)|en|Rabbit (zodiac)|WD=Q844723|lt=Day of the Rabbit|1=Rabbit (zodiac)}} in the eleventh month of the [[Japanese calendar|old lunar calendar]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}
Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date has been fixed on {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which corresponds to the modern public holiday {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which was introduced in 1948.
As a ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|WD=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}'', the name of the ritual is associated with {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter|1=Winter}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998a}}
== Name == ''Niiname-sai'' is the common name of the festival, but the same kanji can also be read ''Jinshō-sai''{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} or ''Niiname-no-Matsuri''.{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} ''Niiname'' can also be read ''Niinae'', ''Niinai'', ''Niwanai'', ''Niwanami'' or ''Nyūnami''.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典">{{Cite web|url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68770|title = 國學院大學 デジタルミュージアム}}</ref>
The first Niiname-sai following the accession of a new emperor is called the ''[[Enthronement of the Japanese Emperor#The Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]]'' (大嘗祭, also read ''Ōname-Matsuri'' and ''Ōnie-no-Matsuri'').{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998}}<!-- Britannica also verifies this, but not the readings, and Britannica's separate article 大嘗祭 actually doesn't give the reading おおなめまつり. -->
== In literature == Book 19 of the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' includes six poems (numbered 4273 to 4278) composed on the 25th day of the eleventh month of 752, the "''Niiname-kai'' poems".<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/> The "''nyūnami''" is alluded to in one of the ''[[azuma-uta]]'' (songs of eastern Japan) included in Book 14.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/>
== See also == * [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Japanese Imperial Rituals]] * [[:en:Saiten|Saiten]]
==Notes== {{notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten |title = Niiname-sai |language = japanese |year = 2014 |publisher = [[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/新嘗祭-109054#E3.83.96.E3.83.AA.E3.82.BF.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.E5.9B.BD.E9.9A.9B.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8.20.E5.B0.8F.E9.A0.85.E7.9B.AE.E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|2014}} }} * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Daijisen]] |title = Shinjō-sai |language = japanese |year = 1998<!-- Couldn't find a date. The edition consulted was the one on Kotobank. Gave it the same date as is cited in the "Ariwara no Narihira" article. --> |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/新嘗祭-109054#E3.83.87.E3.82.B8.E3.82.BF.E3.83.AB.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E6.B3.89 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Digital Daijisen|1998a}} }} * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Daijisen]] |title = Daijō-sai |language = japanese |year = 1998<!-- Couldn't find a date. The edition consulted was the one on Kotobank. Gave it the same date as is cited in the "Ariwara no Narihira" article. --> |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/大嘗祭-39450#E3.83.87.E3.82.B8.E3.82.BF.E3.83.AB.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E6.B3.89 |access-date = 2017-11-08 |ref = {{SfnRef|Digital Daijisen|1998b}} }}

The Engishiki specified imperial involvement with four festivals, the [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]], the two [[Ōharae-shiki]]s and Niiname-no-Matsuri for tribute.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016-11-11 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |edition=Illustrated |location=New York |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|36}}
In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and Niiname-sai in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>

It is held in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]],{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} as well as other shrines including [[Ise Grand Shrine]]{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}

Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date has been fixed on {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which corresponds to the modern public holiday {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which was introduced in 1948.
As a ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|WD=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}'', the name of the ritual is associated with {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter|1=Winter}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998a}}
== Name == ''Niiname-sai'' is the common name of the festival, but the same kanji can also be read ''Jinshō-sai''{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} or ''Niiname-no-Matsuri''.{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} ''Niiname'' can also be read ''Niinae'', ''Niinai'', ''Niwanai'', ''Niwanami'' or ''Nyūnami''.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典">{{Cite web|url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68770|title = 國學院大學 デジタルミュージアム}}</ref>
The first Niiname-sai following the accession of a new emperor is called the ''[[Enthronement of the Japanese Emperor#The Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]]'' (大嘗祭, also read ''Ōname-Matsuri'' and ''Ōnie-no-Matsuri'').{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998}}<!-- Britannica also verifies this, but not the readings, and Britannica's separate article 大嘗祭 actually doesn't give the reading おおなめまつり. -->
== In literature == Book 19 of the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' includes six poems (numbered 4273 to 4278) composed on the 25th day of the eleventh month of 752, the "''Niiname-kai'' poems".<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/> The "''nyūnami''" is alluded to in one of the ''[[azuma-uta]]'' (songs of eastern Japan) included in Book 14.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/>

In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and Niiname-sai in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Ceremony == During the Niiname-sai, an ancient Shinto ritual that says thanks for the crops of the previous year{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and prays for fruitfulness in the following year,{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} says thanks to his [[kami|gods]] for the fall harvest. It is held in the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]],{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} as well as other shrines including [[Ise Grand Shrine]]{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}
== Date == Traditionally, it was held on the last {{ill|Rabbit (zodiac)|en|Rabbit (zodiac)|WD=Q844723|lt=Day of the Rabbit|1=Rabbit (zodiac)}} in the eleventh month of the [[Japanese calendar|old lunar calendar]].{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}}
Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the date has been fixed on {{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which corresponds to the modern public holiday {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|WD=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}},{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} which was introduced in 1948.
As a ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|WD=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}'', the name of the ritual is associated with {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter|1=Winter}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998a}}
== Name == ''Niiname-sai'' is the common name of the festival, but the same kanji can also be read ''Jinshō-sai''{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} or ''Niiname-no-Matsuri''.{{sfnm|1a1=Britannica Kokusai Dai-Hyakkajiten|1y=2014}} ''Niiname'' can also be read ''Niinae'', ''Niinai'', ''Niwanai'', ''Niwanami'' or ''Nyūnami''.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典">{{Cite web|url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68770|title = 國學院大學 デジタルミュージアム}}</ref>
The first Niiname-sai following the accession of a new emperor is called the ''[[Enthronement of the Japanese Emperor#The Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]]'' (大嘗祭, also read ''Ōname-Matsuri'' and ''Ōnie-no-Matsuri'').{{sfnm|1a1=Digital Daijisen|1y=1998}}<!-- Britannica also verifies this, but not the readings, and Britannica's separate article 大嘗祭 actually doesn't give the reading おおなめまつり. -->
== In literature == Book 19 of the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' includes six poems (numbered 4273 to 4278) composed on the 25th day of the eleventh month of 752, the "''Niiname-kai'' poems".<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/> The "''nyūnami''" is alluded to in one of the ''[[azuma-uta]]'' (songs of eastern Japan) included in Book 14.<ref name="万葉神事語辞典"/>
